ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pepper-salt grilled lamb leg. FOODON_03311349 (lamb food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked sheep/lamb meat as the nearest parent. No specific code exists for pepper-salt marinated grilled lamb leg.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227197003 represents lamb as a substance; no specific concept for this preparation exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of pepper salt lamb leg is approximately 200 g, representing a single bone-in or boneless grilled lamb leg portion as served at an Indian restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton (Goat/Lamb), cooked, roasted — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN values for cooked roasted mutton/lamb are used as the closest approved primary source for this Indian-cuisine grilled lamb leg dish. Values are adapted to reflect a grilled preparation with pepper-salt seasoning; sodium is adjusted upward to account for added salt in the marinade. No direct entry for 'pepper salt lamb leg' exists in ICMR-NIN; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Lamb, leg, whole (shank and sirloin), cooked, roasted' (NDB 17083) is used as a cross-reference for micronutrient values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure meat dishes with negligible carbohydrate content (carbohydrate_g < 1 g per 100 g) have an effectively zero glycemic response. Per Veritas v2 convention for very-low-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) as this food contains insufficient carbohydrate to produce a measurable glycemic response; gi_category is 'low'. Glycemic load is calculated based on this GI and the carbohydrate content.

hypertension.key_factor: Added salt in marinade elevates sodium content to approximately 380 mg per 100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced-salt preparation, as a 200 g serving contributes approximately 760 mg sodium, representing about one-third of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content (0.5 g per 100 g) produces no meaningful glycemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pepper salt lamb leg is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate option; however, portion size should be moderated to manage total caloric and saturated fat intake, and the dish should be paired with non-starchy vegetables rather than refined carbohydrate sides.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (4.8 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (95 mg per 100 g) are clinically relevant for cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to one moderate serving (100–150 g) per occasion and choose lean cuts where possible, as a 200 g serving provides approximately 9.6 g saturated fat, approaching the recommended daily limit of 10–15 g for cardiovascular risk management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pepper Salt Lamb Leg (also listed as 'Leg Pepper Salt' on menus) is an Indian-cuisine dish consisting of a lamb leg portion that has been marinated in a blend of black pepper, salt, and aromatic spices before being grilled to produce a charred, flavourful exterior with a tender, juicy interior. The dish is a staple of North Indian and South Indian restaurant menus in Singapore and is associated with the tandoor and open-flame grilling traditions of the Indian subcontinent. The flavour profile is bold and savoury, dominated by coarsely ground black pepper and salt, with underlying notes from the spice marinade. The texture is firm yet succulent, with a slightly crisp outer crust from the grilling process. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (approximately 26.5 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (12 g per 100 g, of which 4.8 g is saturated), and contains negligible carbohydrate (0.5 g per 100 g), making it a low-glycemic food. It is a good source of zinc, vitamin B12, niacin, and iron. The sodium content is elevated due to the salt marinade (approximately 380 mg per 100 g), which is clinically relevant for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is typically halal-certified when served at Indian Muslim or halal-certified restaurants in Singapore. A standard restaurant serving is approximately 200 g.
common_user_queries: Is pepper salt lamb leg healthy?
How many calories in grilled lamb leg?
Is lamb leg good for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of grilled lamb?
Is pepper salt lamb leg halal?
What is the GI of lamb?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for 'Pepper Salt Lamb Leg' exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked mutton/lamb data cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy lamb leg roasted values (NDB 17083). Sodium is adjusted upward to reflect added salt in the marinade. GI is assigned as 1 (effectively zero) based on negligible carbohydrate content per Veritas v2 convention for very-low-carbohydrate foods.
notes: 1. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: lamb meat contains no free sugars; confirmed by ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy data. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: pure meat dish with no plant-based fibre-containing ingredients in the base preparation. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: cooked lamb meat contains no vitamin C per USDA SR Legacy NDB 17083. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 — confirmed zero: lamb leg meat contains negligible retinol activity equivalents per USDA SR Legacy. 5. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this food contains insufficient carbohydrate (0.5 g per 100 g) to produce a measurable glycemic response; gi_category is 'low' per Veritas v2 convention for very-low-carbohydrate foods. glycemic_load_per_serving is calculated as 0.01 based on GI=1 and 1.0 g carbohydrate per serving. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy NDB 17083 (lamb leg, whole, cooked, roasted) as a cross-reference. 7. The dish is served at Anjana Kitchen – Six Battery Road, Singapore (field survey data source). 8. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases list.

improvement_actions_taken: Renamed food_name from 'Leg Pepper Salt' (menu-style) to canonical 'Pepper Salt Lamb Leg'.
Added mandatory multilingual local_names: Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Built comprehensive aliases and aliases_by_language with full consistency check.
Sourced nutrient values from ICMR-NIN (primary) and USDA SR Legacy NDB 17083 (cross-reference).
Documented all zero values (sugar_g, dietary_fibre_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae) with 'confirmed zero' justification in notes.
Corrected `glycemic_index` from 0 to 1 and `glycemic_load_per_serving` from 0.0 to 0.01 as per validator feedback and Veritas v2 rule for non-zero GI.
Adjusted `gi_source.source_match_notes` and `notes` to reflect the GI=1 change and justification.
Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
Selected honest ontology codes with closest_parent match_type and gap notes.
Corrected `aliases_by_language` by moving Tamil script alias from 'romanized' to 'tamil' array for accuracy and consistency.
Verified aliases_by_language to flat aliases consistency before finalising.
